coreMessage: You do not need a tribal ID to begin learning. Cultural reconnection starts with humility, honest investigation, and respectful engagement. The formal relationship with a nation — enrollment, citizenship — is a separate and parallel process. Both matter. Neither blocks the other from starting.

approachPrinciples.principle: Come as a learner, not as a claimant
approachPrinciples.explanation: Enter cultural spaces as someone there to learn, not to have your ancestry confirmed or validated. Elders and community members are not in a position to verify your ancestry claims or to welcome you formally into a community. They may, however, be generous teachers when approached with honesty and humility.

approachPrinciples.principle: Lead with what you don't know
approachPrinciples.explanation: The most credible opening is honesty about the limits of your knowledge. 'I am still investigating my ancestry and am here to learn' is more trustworthy than an unverifiable claim of connection.

At cultural events and in community spaces, the first priority is to receive, not to share. Arrive early. Stay late. Watch how people interact. Ask simple questions about what you are seeing, not about your own ancestry.

Cultural centers and language programs depend on community support. If you benefit from their resources, look for ways to reciprocate: attend fundraising events, purchase community-made goods, volunteer for non-ceremonial tasks.

immersionTypes.title: Public Cultural Events
immersionTypes.description: Many nations host events open to the public — powwows, cultural festivals, canoe journeys, language days. These are opportunities to experience culture in its living form, not as museum artifact.
examples: Tribal canoe journey events (Pacific Northwest, public landings)
Powwows with public welcome (check individual event protocols)
Cultural festival days hosted by cultural centers
immersionTypes.protocolNote: Check event-specific protocols. Some events are open to the public for all activities. Some have portions for community members only. Follow the guidance of event hosts. Do not photograph or record without explicit permission.

immersionTypes.title: Cultural Center and Museum Visits
immersionTypes.description: Cultural centers operated by nations are different from colonial museums. The cultural center is community-built, community-curated, and often staffed by community members. Your experience in a cultural center is closer to the community than a mainstream museum.
examples: U'mista Cultural Centre (Alert Bay — Kwakwaka'wakw regalia)
Smithsonian National Museum of the American Indian (Washington DC and NYC)
State and provincial museums with strong First Nations collections
immersionTypes.protocolNote: When visiting, engage with staff. Explain (briefly, without burdening them) that you are investigating your ancestry. Ask what resources or contacts they recommend. Do not make this the center of the visit — you are there to learn, not to receive.

immersionTypes.title: Language Learning
immersionTypes.description: Learning even basic vocabulary in your ancestral nation's language is a concrete act of reconnection that does not require enrollment documentation. Language learning shows commitment and opens doors. It also takes years — this is not a quick project.
examples: FirstVoices community language archives (many have beginner materials)
University of Victoria Indigenous language programs (open enrollment)
Local friendship centers with language programs
Nation-specific language nests or learning programs (contact cultural center)
immersionTypes.protocolNote: Approach language learning with patience and humility. You are learning a living language that belongs to a community, not a historical artifact. If a community language program is for members only, respect that. Start with what is publicly available and build from there.

immersionTypes.title: Heritage Skills Learning
immersionTypes.description: The Heritage Skills Educational Pack itself is a culturally appropriate starting point. Content is organized by cultural sovereignty level — publicly shared techniques (Level 1-2) are available to learners at any stage of ancestry investigation. Heritage skills practice is a form of cultural engagement that does not require enrollment.
examples: Try Sessions in the Skill Hall (all publicly shared content)
Cedar Culture room (bark techniques, canoe types)
Salish Weaving room (two-bar loom, cedar basketry)
Village World room (potlatch-as-technology, emotional intelligence frameworks)
immersionTypes.protocolNote: The pack's cultural sovereignty classifications guide what is appropriate to learn at this stage. Level 1 content is publicly shared. Level 2 content is contextually shared with appropriate framing. Level 3-4 content is restricted — the pack will tell you when you have reached a boundary.

protocolGuidance.introductionPhrase: I am of Coast Salish ancestry through my biological line, but I was separated by adoption and am now seeking to learn the history of my ancestors.
protocolGuidance.whenToUseIt: Use this phrase when approaching cultural centers, language programs, or community elders for the first time. It establishes honest context. It does not guarantee welcome — but it establishes honest ground.
protocolGuidance.followUp: After introducing yourself with the protocol phrase, ask: 'What resources do you recommend for someone in my situation?' Let the community member guide you. Do not ask them to verify your ancestry or to welcome you formally — those are separate processes that take time.

whatToExpect.warmth: Many community members and cultural center staff are genuinely supportive of reconnecting descendants. This work is recognized and valued by many Indigenous organizations.
whatToExpect.caution: Some community members will be cautious about unverified ancestry claims. This is understandable and appropriate — communities have experience with people claiming ancestry opportunistically. The caution is not rejection; it is a reasonable boundary.
whatToExpect.grief: Your own reconnection process may bring up unexpected grief — for the culture you did not grow up with, for family members who could have told you things they didn't, for the circumstances that caused the separation. This is normal. The Sixties Scoop Network and similar organizations have peer support resources for this.
whatToExpect.patience: Cultural reconnection is not a project with a finish line. It is a relationship that develops over time. Expect the process to take years. Expect to make mistakes. Expect to learn from them.
